description:
Implement subagent wrapup in the OpenCode plugin using tool.execute.after filtered to tool="task".

## Research Findings (confirmed via DeepWiki + OpenCode source)

The original assumption that session.idle was the only option was WRONG. OpenCode has a clean path:

### How it works
- tool.execute.after fires with tool="task" when a TaskTool (subagent) completes
- The payload includes:
  - title: task description
  - output: final text output from subagent (may include <task_metadata> block with session ID)
  - metadata.summary: summary of tool calls within the subagent session
  - metadata.sessionID: the subagent session ID
  - metadata.model: model used by subagent
  - attachments: optional FilePart array

### Implementation approach
In plugin.js, add a filter in the tool.execute.after handler:

```javascript
if (input.tool === "task") {
  // This is a subagent completion
  // 1. Check output/metadata.summary for BEAD_ID pattern
  // 2. Check if subagent logged any knowledge comments (bd comments add)
  // 3. If BEAD_ID found but no knowledge logged, inject reminder
  //    (cannot block like Claude Code SubagentStop, but can log warning)
}
```

### Key differences from Claude Code SubagentStop
1. Cannot "block" completion - tool.execute.after is post-execution, not a gate
2. CAN inspect the full subagent output and tool call summary
3. CAN inject a follow-up system message reminding to log knowledge
4. CAN log a warning comment on the bead automatically

### Behavioral design
Since we cannot block (like subagent-wrapup.sh does with decision: block), the OpenCode version should:
1. Parse output + metadata.summary for BEAD_ID references
2. Check if any bd comments add calls appear in the summary
3. If BEAD_ID found but no knowledge captured:
   - Auto-add a reminder comment to the bead: "WARNING: Subagent completed without logging knowledge"
   - Return output with appended reminder text via the hook output
4. If knowledge WAS captured, no action needed

### Integration with plugin.js
This is an additional filter branch in the existing tool.execute.after handler (already needed for memory-capture). Estimated: ~20 lines added to plugin.js.

## Files to modify
- plugins/beads-compound/opencode/plugin.js (add task tool filter branch)
title: Implement subagent wrapup via tool.execute.after (tool=task)
Document dotfiles + plugin global install compatibility issues

description:
Add cross-platform compatibility to the beads-compound plugin, starting with OpenCode and Gemini CLI.

## Platforms

### OpenCode (TypeScript plugin)
- Plugin system: TypeScript files in .opencode/plugins/ (Bun-native, no build step)
- Event mapping: session.created, tool.execute.after, shell.env
- Subagent wrapup: tool.execute.after with tool="task" filter (confirmed via DeepWiki)
- Reads: AGENTS.md
- Install: manual copy of plugin.ts + package.json

### Gemini CLI (settings.json hooks)
- Hook system: Nearly identical to Claude Code (JSON stdin/stdout wire protocol)
- Event mapping: SessionStart, AfterTool, AfterAgent
- Has built-in Claude Code migration path in source
- Reads: GEMINI.md (configurable via context.fileName)
- Install: `gemini extensions install <github-url>` (needs gemini-extension.json manifest)

### Not yet viable
- Codex CLI: Hook system not shipped (PR #11067 closed). Similar wire protocol to Claude Code when it lands.
- Antigravity: No hook/lifecycle event system. Skills-only extension model.

## Shared foundation
- Shell scripts (auto-recall.sh, memory-capture.sh, subagent-wrapup.sh) are already portable
- Knowledge system (.beads/memory/knowledge.jsonl) is tool-agnostic
- bd CLI works in any terminal

## Key decisions
- Use Bun.spawn() not $ shell template in plugin.ts (security)
- Pre-fork filter bd comments? add regex in TypeScript before spawning subprocess (performance)
- Pin @opencode-ai/plugin to exact version (supply chain)
- AGENTS.md is open standard adopted by 20+ tools; symlink CLAUDE.md -> AGENTS.md recommended
title: Epic: Multi-platform support (OpenCode, Gemini CLI)

description:
## What

The /beads-brainstorm command currently only accepts feature descriptions as arguments. It should also support bead IDs to allow users to brainstorm on existing beads (especially vague epics that need more exploration before planning child beads).

## Expected Behavior

The command should:
1. Detect if the argument is a bead ID (matches pattern like 'beads-*' or alphanumeric segments with hyphens)
2. If bead ID detected, load bead details with `bd show {ID} --json` and extract the description
3. Use the bead's title and description as context for brainstorming
4. If not a bead ID, proceed with current behavior (treat as feature description)

## Context

- Similar to the fix applied to /beads-plan (bdcompound-nab)
- Use case: User has vague epic and wants to explore it more deeply before creating child beads
- Pattern: ^[a-z0-9]+-[a-z0-9]+(-[a-z0-9]+)*$

## Testing

- [ ] Test with epic bead ID: `/beads-brainstorm {EPIC_ID}`
- [ ] Test with feature description: `/beads-brainstorm "Add user notifications"`
- [ ] Verify bead content is loaded when ID is passed
- [ ] Verify feature description is used when non-ID text is passed
- [ ] Handle invalid bead IDs gracefully

## Validation

- [ ] Command correctly identifies bead IDs vs feature descriptions
- [ ] Bead content is extracted and used for brainstorming when ID is passed
- [ ] Original feature description behavior still works
- [ ] Error handling for invalid/non-existent bead IDs
title: Support bead ID argument in /beads-brainstorm command

description:
Refactor install.sh and uninstall.sh to be thin dispatchers that route to platform-specific installer scripts.

## Architecture

Main scripts become routers:
- install.sh: Parse flags, default to -claude, exec installers/install-<platform>.sh
- uninstall.sh: Parse flags, default to -claude, exec installers/uninstall-<platform>.sh

## Structure

```
installers/
├── install-claude.sh      # Current install.sh logic (541 lines)
├── install-opencode.sh    # .opencode/plugins/, commands/, skills/, agents/
├── install-gemini.sh      # Gemini extension files
├── uninstall-claude.sh    # Current uninstall.sh logic
├── uninstall-opencode.sh  # Remove .opencode/ files
└── uninstall-gemini.sh    # Remove Gemini extension
```

## Implementation

### 1. Move existing logic
```bash
# Move current install.sh to installers/install-claude.sh
mv install.sh installers/install-claude.sh
mv uninstall.sh installers/uninstall-claude.sh
```

### 2. Create thin dispatchers
install.sh:
```bash
#!/bin/bash
PLATFORM="claude"  # Default for backwards compatibility

case "$1" in
  -claude|--claude)
    PLATFORM="claude"
    shift
    ;;
  -opencode|--opencode)
    PLATFORM="opencode"
    shift
    ;;
  -gemini|--gemini)
    PLATFORM="gemini"
    shift
    ;;
  -h|--help)
    echo "Usage: ./install.sh [-claude|-opencode|-gemini] [options]"
    echo "Default: -claude"
    exit 0
    ;;
esac

exec "$(dirname "$0")/installers/install-${PLATFORM}.sh" "$@"
```

uninstall.sh follows same pattern.

### 3. Create OpenCode installer (blocked by bdcompound-xr3)
installers/install-opencode.sh:
- Copy plugin.ts + package.json to .opencode/plugins/beads-compound/
- Run bun install
- Copy hooks/
- Copy commands/ to .opencode/commands/ (once available)
- Copy skills/ to .opencode/skills/ (once available)
- Copy agents/ to .opencode/agents/ (once available)
- Security: validate .opencode/ is not a symlink
- Set restrictive permissions

### 4. Create Gemini installer
installers/install-gemini.sh:
- Instructions to run: gemini extensions install <github-url>
- Or manual: copy gemini-extension.json, gemini/settings.json
- Copy hooks/ if manual install

## Benefits

- Clean separation of platform logic
- Easy to add new platforms (just add new script)
- Each installer testable independently
- No complex branching in main scripts
- Backwards compatible (default to -claude)

## Testing

```bash
# Should work as before (default Claude)
./install.sh
./install.sh /path/to/project

# New platform flags
./install.sh -opencode
./install.sh -gemini
./uninstall.sh -opencode /path/to/project
```
notes:
## SECURITY REQUIREMENTS (from plan review)

### Critical Security Controls

1. **Platform Flag Validation**
   - Use strict allowlist validation (claude|opencode|gemini only)
   - Prevent command injection via platform flag
   - Implementation:
     ```bash
     validate_platform() {
       local platform="$1"
       case "$platform" in
         claude|opencode|gemini) return 0 ;;
         *)
           echo "[\!] Error: Invalid platform '$platform'"
           echo "    Allowed: claude, opencode, gemini"
           exit 1
           ;;
       esac
     }
     ```

2. **Installer Path Security**
   - Verify installer exists and is in expected directory
   - Use path canonicalization:
     ```bash
     INSTALLER="$SCRIPT_DIR/installers/install-${PLATFORM}.sh"
     INSTALLER_REAL="$(realpath "$INSTALLER")"
     EXPECTED_DIR="$(realpath "$SCRIPT_DIR/installers")"
     
     if [[ \! "$INSTALLER_REAL" =~ ^"$EXPECTED_DIR"/install-[a-z]+\.sh$ ]]; then
       echo "[\!] Error: Installer path validation failed"
       exit 1
     fi
     ```

3. **Directory Creation Safety**
   - Prevent TOCTOU race conditions
   - Validate ownership and symlink checks:
     ```bash
     create_safe_dir() {
       local dir="$1"
       mkdir -m 700 "$dir" || return 1
       
       # Verify not a symlink
       if [[ -L "$dir" ]]; then
         echo "[\!] Error: Directory is a symlink"
         return 1
       fi
       
       # Verify ownership
       local owner=$(stat -f%Su "$dir" 2>/dev/null || stat -c%U "$dir")
       if [[ "$owner" \!= "$USER" ]]; then
         echo "[\!] Error: Directory owned by different user"
         return 1
       fi
     }
     ```

4. **File Copy Validation**
   - Allowlist file types before copy
   - Scan for suspicious patterns
   - No executables in data directories:
     ```bash
     safe_copy_directory() {
       local src="$1" dst="$2"
       mkdir -p "$dst"
       
       # Copy only approved file types
       find "$src" -type f \( -name "*.md" -o -name "*.json" -o -name "*.yaml" \) \
         -exec cp {} "$dst/" \;
       
       # Verify no executables
       if find "$dst" -type f -perm +111 | grep -q .; then
         echo "[\!] Error: Executable files detected"
         rm -rf "$dst"
         return 1
       fi
     }
     ```

5. **MCP Config Merge Security**
   - Validate JSON structure before merge
   - Check for prototype pollution (__proto__, constructor)
   - Backup existing config:
     ```bash
     validate_mcp_config() {
       local config="$1"
       
       # Check for prototype pollution
       if echo "$config" | jq -e '.mcpServers | has("__proto__")' | grep -q true; then
         echo "[\!] Error: Prototype pollution detected"
         return 1
       fi
       
       # Validate structure
       if \! echo "$config" | jq -e '.mcpServers | type == "object"' > /dev/null; then
         echo "[\!] Error: Invalid MCP config structure"
         return 1
       fi
     }
     
     # Backup before merge
     cp "$target_config" "${target_config}.backup"
     ```

6. **Permission Hardening**
   - Set restrictive umask: `umask 077`
   - Explicit permissions after file operations:
     ```bash
     # Commands: read-only
     find "$COMMANDS_DIR" -type f -exec chmod 644 {} \;
     
     # Hooks: executable
     find "$HOOKS_DIR" -type f -name "*.sh" -exec chmod 755 {} \;
     
     # Skills: read-only
     find "$SKILLS_DIR" -type f -name "SKILL.md" -exec chmod 444 {} \;
     ```

### Implementation Checklist

- [ ] Platform flag allowlist validation
- [ ] Installer path canonicalization check
- [ ] Safe directory creation (TOCTOU protection)
- [ ] File type allowlist for copies
- [ ] MCP config validation and backup
- [ ] Explicit file permissions (644/755)
- [ ] Umask set to 077 before operations

### Testing Requirements

- [ ] Test invalid platform flags (../../../etc/passwd)
- [ ] Test symlink directory attacks
- [ ] Test malicious MCP configs (prototype pollution)
- [ ] Verify file permissions after install
title: Refactor install.sh/uninstall.sh to use platform-specific installers

description:
## Issue
execSync calls in bin/plan-export.js and bin/plan-view.js interpolate bead IDs directly into shell commands without validation. A bead ID containing shell metacharacters could execute arbitrary commands.

## Severity
P1 CRITICAL - Command injection via user-controlled input

## Location
- bin/plan-export.js: execSync(`bd show ${beadId}`) pattern
- bin/plan-view.js: execSync(`bd list --parent ${beadId}`) pattern

## Why This Matters
Any user-provided argument passed to the CLI could inject shell commands. While bead IDs are typically auto-generated, users can pass arbitrary strings as arguments.

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] bead ID validated against /^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+$/ before shell interpolation
- [ ] OR use execFileSync with array args instead of execSync with template strings
- [ ] Malicious input like 'BD-001; rm -rf /' rejected or safely passed

## Testing Steps
1. Run: bd-plan-view 'BD-001; echo INJECTED'
2. Expected: validation error, not command execution
title: Command injection in plan-export.js and plan-view.js via unvalidated bead IDs

description:
## Issue
In .github/workflows/test-installation.yml, `node $GITHUB_WORKSPACE/bin/install.js` is unquoted. If a self-hosted runner has spaces in the workspace path, this splits into multiple shell tokens and fails.

## Severity
P2 — Latent failure on self-hosted runners

## Location
.github/workflows/test-installation.yml (npx installer job, node invocation line)

## Fix
```yaml
node "$GITHUB_WORKSPACE/bin/install.js" --claude --yes
```

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] All $GITHUB_WORKSPACE references in shell steps are quoted
title: GITHUB_WORKSPACE unquoted in CI — breaks on self-hosted runners with spaces in path

description:
## Issue
.github/workflows/npm-publish.yml publishes without:
1. `--provenance` flag (SLSA attestation linking package to CI run/commit)
2. `--access public` flag (explicit scope for scoped packages)

## Severity
P2 — Supply chain hygiene gap; no cryptographic link from published package to source

## Location
.github/workflows/npm-publish.yml: npm publish step

## Fix
Add to job permissions: `id-token: write`
Change publish command: `npm publish --provenance --access public`

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] Published package shows provenance attestation on npmjs.com
- [ ] --access public prevents accidental private publish for scoped packages
title: npm publish missing --provenance and --access public flags

description:
## Issue
bin/plan-view.js registers `process.on('exit', cleanup)` but all exit paths already call cleanup() explicitly. The handler fires redundantly on every exit.

## Location
bin/plan-view.js: process.on('exit') registration

## Fix
Remove the `process.on('exit', cleanup)` registration. Keep the explicit cleanup() calls before each process.exit().

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] cleanup() called exactly once per exit
- [ ] Terminal state correctly restored in all exit scenarios
title: plan-view.js redundant process.on('exit', cleanup) registration

description:
## Issue
The npx smoke test job in .github/workflows/test-installation.yml runs the beads-compound installer which calls install.sh, which calls 'bd init'. The bd CLI is not installed in CI, so bd init fails with a non-zero exit and the entire installer fails.

## Severity
P1 CRITICAL - Blocks CI, every commit fails the test suite

## Location
- .github/workflows/test-installation.yml: test-npx-installer job
- install.sh: bd init call

## Why This Matters
The CI gate cannot pass, making the npm-publish workflow unreliable and blocking confidence in releases.

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] CI test-npx-installer job passes green
- [ ] Either install bd in CI before running the installer, OR skip bd init in non-interactive CI

## Testing Steps
1. Push to branch and observe test-installation.yml run
2. Expected: test-npx-installer passes
title: npx smoke test fails in CI — bd not installed in CI environment

close_reason: Restored process.on('exit', cleanup). Investigation showed die() calls precede setRawMode in current code, but the handler is valuable defensive practice for future changes.
description:
## Issue
The die() function in bin/plan-view.js calls process.exit(1) without first calling cleanup(). When buildTree fails (e.g., bead not found), the terminal is left in raw mode with the cursor hidden, requiring the user to run reset or stty sane to recover.

## Severity
P1 CRITICAL — Corrupts the user's terminal on bead lookup failure.

## Location
- bin/plan-view.js:451 — die() calls process.exit(1) with no cleanup
- bin/plan-view.js:134 — die() called from buildTree after raw mode is already set (line 487)

## Root Cause
process.on('exit', cleanup) was removed in commit 873d9f1. Previously it was the catch-all. Now cleanup() is only called from keyboard handlers. The die() path bypasses all keyboard handlers.

## Why This Matters
Any user running bd-plan-view BD-NONEXISTENT will get a corrupted terminal. Raw mode stays on, cursor stays hidden. This is the most common error path.

## Fix Options
1. Restore process.on('exit', cleanup) (one line, no side effects)
2. Call cleanup() inside die() before process.exit(1)

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] Running bd-plan-view BD-NONEXISTENT does not leave terminal in broken state
- [ ] Cursor is visible after exit
- [ ] Terminal input echoing works normally after failed lookup

## Testing Steps
1. Run: node bin/plan-view.js BD-NONEXISTENT
2. Expected: error message, terminal fully restored
3. NOT expected: cursor missing, input echoing broken
title: plan-view.js: die() doesn't call cleanup() — terminal left in broken state

description:
## Issue
In .github/workflows/test-installation.yml, the 'Run installation tests' step has continue-on-error: true. This means even when beads is available and tests actually run, a genuine test failure does not fail CI. The step also explicitly does exit 0 in its catch block, creating a double guard that guarantees CI always stays green regardless of test outcome.

## Severity
P1 CRITICAL — Real installation regressions pass CI silently.

## Location
- .github/workflows/test-installation.yml: 'Run installation tests' step (~line 57-70)

## Why This Matters
Making a step conditional (if: steps.check-beads.outputs.available == 'true') correctly handles the optional external dependency. But adding continue-on-error: true on top means: when beads IS available and tests run, failures are still ignored. This defeats the purpose of the test step.

## Fix
Remove continue-on-error: true from the 'Run installation tests' step. The conditional guard already handles beads being unavailable. If beads is installed and the test fails, that should be a real CI failure.

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] Removing continue-on-error and running CI confirms green on a correct installation
- [ ] A deliberate test failure (e.g., break a directory path assertion) causes CI to go red

## Testing Steps
1. Remove continue-on-error: true from the step
2. Push and verify green run
3. Introduce deliberate failure, verify CI goes red
title: CI: continue-on-error on installation test step swallows real failures

description:
## Issue
The npm-publish workflow has no needs: verify-release dependency. This means npm publish can run and succeed while the verify-release job (which checks version consistency, component counts, and conversion outputs) is still running or has failed.

## Severity
P2 IMPORTANT — Can publish a broken or incorrectly-versioned package to npm.

## Location
- .github/workflows/npm-publish.yml: publish job has no needs: clause referencing verify-release

## Why This Matters
The verify-release job exists precisely to gate releases. Running npm-publish without waiting for it means the gate can be bypassed on any release. A version mismatch or missing conversion outputs would result in a published package that fails to install correctly.

## Fix
Add needs: [verify-release] to the publish job in npm-publish.yml. This requires that verify-release runs first and passes before npm publish executes.

Note: verify-release is in test-installation.yml which is a separate workflow. Cross-workflow dependencies may require using workflow_run trigger or a combined workflow. Evaluate the simplest approach.

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] npm-publish does not run if verify-release fails
- [ ] CI order is deterministic: verify first, publish second

## Testing Steps
1. Add needs dependency
2. Create a test release tag
3. Verify verify-release runs before publish
title: npm-publish.yml: no needs:verify-release — can publish while CI verification failing

description:
## Issue
Both npx installer test jobs in test-installation.yml install the beads CLI via:
  curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/steveyegge/beads/main/scripts/install.sh | bash

This pins to the HEAD of the main branch, not to a specific commit SHA. If the steveyegge/beads repo is compromised, renamed, or its install script is maliciously modified, arbitrary code runs in the CI runner with access to NPM_TOKEN and other repo secrets.

## Severity
P2 IMPORTANT — Supply chain risk in CI environment.

## Location
- .github/workflows/test-installation.yml: Install beads CLI steps (~lines 55, 110)

## Why This Matters
curl|bash from a mutable URL is the canonical supply chain attack vector. The CI runner has NPM_TOKEN in scope.

## Fix Options
1. Pin to a specific commit SHA: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/steveyegge/beads/<SHA>/scripts/install.sh
2. Vendor the install script into this repo and use a local path
3. Add a checksum verification step after download before execution

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] The install script URL is pinned to a specific SHA or checksum-verified
- [ ] A compromised HEAD of steveyegge/beads main does not affect this CI

## Testing Steps
1. Pin the URL to a known-good SHA
2. Verify CI still passes with the pinned URL
title: CI: curl|bash to install beads has no SHA pin — supply chain risk

description:
## Issue
scripts/pre-release-check.sh checks OpenCode and Gemini command counts at >= 25, but .github/workflows/test-installation.yml (verify-release job) checks >= 29. Both files have a SYNC: comment requiring them to stay in sync. They are out of sync.

## Severity
P2 IMPORTANT — The local pre-release script gives a false pass (25 is achievable with 4 missing commands), while CI will fail. This makes the pre-release script useless as a local gate.

## Location
- scripts/pre-release-check.sh: lines 105-106 (threshold 25 for OpenCode and Gemini)
- .github/workflows/test-installation.yml: lines 201, 204 (threshold 29)

## Fix
Update scripts/pre-release-check.sh OpenCode and Gemini thresholds from 25 to 29 to match CI.

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] pre-release-check.sh and test-installation.yml use the same threshold (29) for all three output formats
- [ ] Running pre-release-check.sh locally gives the same pass/fail result as CI would

## Testing Steps
1. Update lines 105-106 in pre-release-check.sh to use 29
2. Run: bash scripts/pre-release-check.sh
3. Verify it passes (same as CI would)
title: pre-release-check.sh OpenCode/Gemini thresholds stuck at 25 (CI uses 29) — SYNC violation

description:
## Issue
bdExecFile in bin/plan-view.js uses timeout: 10000 (10 seconds) while the equivalent function in bin/plan-export.js uses timeout: 30000 (30 seconds). On a slow system or large bead tree, a 10s timeout causes bdExecFile to silently return empty string, which plan-view.js interprets as a missing bead — silently dropping phases or tasks from the displayed tree.

## Severity
P2 IMPORTANT — Silent data loss: users see an incomplete tree with no error indication.

## Location
- bin/plan-view.js:65 — timeout: 10000
- bin/plan-export.js:82 — timeout: 30000

## Why This Matters
The failure mode in plan-view.js is worse than in plan-export.js. In plan-export.js a timeout aborts with an error message. In plan-view.js the error is silently swallowed (bdExecFile returns empty string), so the TUI shows a truncated tree as if the missing beads don't exist. Users have no way to know data is missing.

## Fix
Raise timeout in plan-view.js bdExecFile to 30000ms to match plan-export.js.

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] Both plan-view.js and plan-export.js use timeout: 30000
- [ ] On a slow system, plan-view waits longer rather than silently truncating

## Testing Steps
1. Update timeout in plan-view.js bdExecFile to 30000
2. Verify plan-view still works normally on fast systems
title: plan-view.js: bdExecFile timeout 10s causes silent tree truncation on slow bd calls

## Issue
validateBeadId() is called at every individual fetch function (fetchBead, fetchChildren, fetchComments in plan-export.js; bdShowJson, bdListChildren, bdCommentsList in plan-view.js) instead of once at the entry point where the bead ID enters the program. This is redundant since execFileSync already prevents injection regardless, and the ID doesn't change between calls.

Additional code cleanup items in the same files:
1. plan-export.js: require statements appear after validateBeadId function definition (lines 15-16 should be at top)
2. plan-view.js: validateBeadId uses process.stderr.write instead of console.error (inconsistent with rest of file)
3. plan-view.js: usage() has no exitCode param unlike plan-export.js — callers must follow up with die() creating double-error output
4. install.js: _positional naming has underscore prefix suggesting 'unused' but is used immediately
5. plan-view.js: SIGINT handler is vestigial in raw mode (Ctrl-C comes through as keypress, not signal)

## Severity
P3 NICE-TO-HAVE — Code clarity and consistency.

## Location
- bin/plan-export.js:92, 103, 113 (redundant validateBeadId calls)
- bin/plan-view.js:75, 86, 98 (redundant validateBeadId calls)
- bin/plan-export.js:15-16 (require placement)
- bin/plan-view.js:11 (stderr.write vs console.error)
- bin/plan-view.js:455-469 (usage() no exitCode)
- bin/install.js:52 (_positional naming)
- bin/plan-view.js:501-508 (vestigial SIGINT handler)

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] validateBeadId called once at top of main execution path
- [ ] require statements grouped at top of plan-export.js
- [ ] Consistent console.error usage in validateBeadId
- [ ] usage() consistent between plan-view.js and plan-export.js

## Testing Steps
1. After changes, test both bin/plan-view.js and bin/plan-export.js with valid and invalid bead IDs
2. Verify --help shows usage and exits 0
3. Verify invalid bead ID exits 1 with clear error
title: Code cleanup: validateBeadId called per-function instead of once at entry

- [ ] Consider adding verify-release trigger on push to main

## Testing Steps

3. Verify CI still passes
title: CI: Cortex conversion not verified in verify-release job

description:
## Issue
In bin/install.js, the uninstall path hardcodes process.cwd() as the uninstall target, ignoring any positional path argument passed by the user.

## Severity
P1 CRITICAL - Silent data loss: user passes a project path expecting that project to be uninstalled, but current directory gets uninstalled instead

## Location
- bin/install.js:236 — `const target = process.cwd();`

## Why This Matters
`npx beads-compound --uninstall /path/to/project` silently uninstalls from the wrong directory.

## Fix
Change the uninstall target to: `const target = TARGET_PATH || process.cwd();`

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] `npx beads-compound --uninstall /tmp/some-project` uninstalls from /tmp/some-project, not cwd
- [ ] `npx beads-compound --uninstall` (no path) still uninstalls from cwd

## Testing Steps
1. Install into /tmp/test-project
2. From a different directory, run: node bin/install.js --uninstall /tmp/test-project
3. Expected: /tmp/test-project uninstalled. cwd unchanged.
title: Uninstall in install.js always uses cwd, ignores TARGET_PATH argument

description:
## Issue
In bin/plan-export.js, bd subprocess errors cause process.exit(1) with no user-facing error message. Users see a blank failure with no indication of what went wrong.

## Severity
P1 CRITICAL - Silent failures that are impossible to debug

## Location
- bin/plan-export.js: error handling in execSync calls

## Why This Matters
When a bead ID doesn't exist or bd is not installed, the user gets no feedback. Debugging is impossible.

## Fix
Catch subprocess errors, print a descriptive message, then exit:
```js
try {
  const output = execSync(`bd show ...`);
} catch (err) {
  console.error(`Error: failed to get bead data. Is bd installed? Details: ${err.message}`);
  process.exit(1);
}
```

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] Running bd-plan-export with a nonexistent bead shows a clear error message
- [ ] Error includes enough info to diagnose (is bd installed? does bead exist?)

## Testing Steps
1. Run: bd-plan-export BD-NONEXISTENT
2. Expected: descriptive error message, exit 1
3. NOT expected: silent exit 1 with no output
title: plan-export.js exits hard on any bd subprocess error, no error message

## Location
- plugins/beads-compound/commands/beads-parallel.md

Without success_criteria, agents executing this command cannot self-verify completion. This undermines the entire purpose of the XML tag standard.

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] beads-parallel.md contains <success_criteria> tag
- [ ] success_criteria covers both --ralph and --teams mode outcomes
- [ ] Tag position follows standard (after process, before guardrails)

## Testing Steps
1. grep -c '<success_criteria>' plugins/beads-compound/commands/beads-parallel.md
2. Expected: 1 (or more for nested)
title: beads-parallel.md missing required <success_criteria> XML tag

description:
## Issue
In bin/install.js:52-54, positional path detection uses `args.find(a => !a.startsWith('-'))`. A path like `-my-project` is silently dropped, falling through to wrong target. Also, `args.find()` is called twice redundantly.

## Severity
P2 — Silent misdirection to wrong install target

## Location
bin/install.js:52-54

## Fix
```js
const positional = args.find((a) => !a.startsWith("-"));
const TARGET_PATH = positional ? path.resolve(positional) : null;
```

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] args.find() called only once
- [ ] Paths starting with '-' produce a clear error rather than silent drop
title: Paths starting with '-' silently treated as flags in install.js TARGET_PATH parsing

close_reason: Implemented as part of holistic redesign
description:
## Problem

The current workflow for thorough feature planning requires running 4 commands manually in
sequence:

1. /beads-brainstorm -- collaborative dialogue exploring WHAT to build, producing a brainstorm
bead with DECISION/INVESTIGATION/FACT/PATTERN comments
2. /beads-plan -- transforms the idea into a structured epic with child task beads, each with
detailed descriptions (What/Context/Testing/Validation/Files/Dependencies)
3. /beads-deepen -- enriches every child bead with parallel multi-agent research (20-40+ agents),
applying skill discoveries, learnings, and review/research/design agent outputs
4. /beads-plan-review -- dispatches 4 review agents (architecture-strategist, code-simplicity-
reviewer, security-sentinel, performance-oracle) to catch issues before implementation

Each command produces artifacts (bead IDs, knowledge comments, enriched descriptions) that the
next command consumes. The user must manually pass context between them and remember the correct
sequence. This friction discourages using the full pipeline.

## Solution

A new /beads-design command that orchestrates the entire planning pipeline as a single
invocation, without skipping steps or losing functionality.

## Architecture Decisions

### Delegation, not duplication

/beads-design is a pure orchestrator that calls the 4 existing commands in sequence. Zero code
duplication. When individual commands are updated or improved, /beads-design automatically
inherits those improvements. The command file itself contains only orchestration logic: phase
transitions, progress banners, verification gates, and the final summary.

### Smart pauses (user interaction model)

• Phase 1 (Brainstorm): Fully interactive -- the brainstorm phase requires collaborative
dialogue (questions, approach selection). Cannot be automated.
• Phase 2 (Plan): Runs automatically after brainstorm completes, consuming brainstorm output. No
user interaction needed.
• Transition pause: After plan completes, the orchestrator shows a plan summary and asks the
user to confirm before investing heavy compute in deepen. This is the single checkpoint between
the interactive and autonomous portions.
• Phase 3+4 (Deepen + Plan-Review): Auto-chains without stopping. Deepen output feeds directly
into plan-review.

### Phase gates with recovery

Each phase runs its existing verification logic. If verification fails at any phase, the
orchestrator pauses and presents three options: retry the phase, skip it and continue, or abort
the pipeline. This prevents wasted compute on downstream phases when an upstream phase has
issues.

### Auto-apply safe feedback

After plan-review runs, non-controversial feedback (typos, missing test cases, documentation
gaps,
straightforward improvements) is applied automatically to child beads. The orchestrator only
pauses for trade-off decisions that require user judgment (e.g., architectural alternatives,
scope changes, performance vs. simplicity trade-offs).

### Default Comprehensive detail level

Since /beads-design is the full-thoroughness pipeline, the plan phase defaults to Comprehensive
detail level. The user can override this inline (e.g., /beads-design standard "feature idea")
but the default matches the intent of using the full pipeline.

### No skip flags, auto-detect context

If the user passes a bead ID that already has brainstorm context (brainstorm label, DECISION
comments), the brainstorm phase detects this and skips itself. No --skip-brainstorm or --
from=plan flags needed. The interface stays simple: /beads-design "feature idea" or /beads-design {bead-
id}.

### Parallelism preserved

No new parallelism constraints introduced. Each delegated command retains its internal
parallelism:

• /beads-plan spawns parallel research agents (repo-research-analyst + learnings-researcher,
optionally best-practices-researcher + framework-docs-researcher)
• /beads-deepen spawns 20-40+ agents across skill discovery, learnings application, per-section
research, and all review/research/design agents
• /beads-plan-review runs 4 review agents concurrently
Model hints (haiku for lightweight research, sonnet for analysis, opus for deep review) are
inherited from existing commands.

### Output style

• Progress banners at each phase transition (e.g., '=== Phase 2/4: Planning... ===')
• Clear verification status per phase (pass/fail with details)
• Final consolidated summary: epic bead ID, child bead count, key decisions captured, review
findings applied, and suggested next step (/beads-work or /beads-parallel)

## Precedent

Follows the /lfg command pattern, which already chains plan -> deepen -> work -> review ->
resolve-todo-parallel -> test-browser -> feature-video as a compound command.

## Usage

/beads-design "feature idea or description" /beads-design {existing-bead-id}

## Success Criteria

• Running /beads-design produces identical artifacts to running the 4 commands manually
• No functionality is lost from any individual command
• User interaction is reduced to: brainstorm dialogue + one plan confirmation + trade-off
decisions only
• Total pipeline context (bead IDs, knowledge comments, enriched descriptions) flows correctly
between all phases
• Phase gate recovery works correctly (retry/skip/abort)
title: Brainstorm: /beads-design unified planning pipeline command

description:
## Vision

Port four improvements from upstream compound-engineering-plugin, adapted for beads ecosystem:
1. project-setup skill — per-project review agent configuration
2. Enhanced planning — Sources section, cross-check validation, brainstorm handoff
3. beads-parallel swarm improvements — dynamic agent discovery, parallel review+test phase
4. migration-drift-detector — generalized for Rails, Alembic, Prisma, Drizzle

## Context

Upstream repo: https://github.com/EveryInc/compound-engineering-plugin (v2.38.1)
Our current version: v0.6.8+

## Non-Requirements

- No ce:* command renaming (our beads-* prefix is fine)
- No cross-platform sync (we handle that differently)
- No proof skill (Every-specific)
title: Upstream sync: project-setup skill, planning enhancements, parallel swarm patterns, migration-drift-detector

description:
## What

Create a new project-setup skill at plugins/beads-compound/skills/project-setup/ that:
1. Auto-detects project tech stack (Rails, Python, TypeScript, JavaScript, Ruby, General)
2. Lets users interactively select which review agents should run
3. Saves config to .beads/config/project-setup.md with YAML frontmatter
4. Provides stack-appropriate defaults
5. Handles monorepo/polyglot projects with multiple stacks

## Context

Upstream uses compound-engineering.local.md in project root. We store in .beads/config/project-setup.md because:
- .beads/memory/ uses merge=union gitattributes which would CORRUPT YAML frontmatter during merges (architecture-strategist finding)
- .beads/config/ is a new directory with clean separation: memory = knowledge data, config = project configuration
- Installer (provision-memory.sh or new provision-config.sh) bootstraps it

Config format:
```markdown
---
stack: python
review_agents:
  - kieran-python-reviewer
  - code-simplicity-reviewer
  - security-sentinel
  - performance-oracle
  - architecture-strategist
plan_review_agents:
  - kieran-python-reviewer
  - code-simplicity-reviewer
---

<reviewer_context_note>
FastAPI project with SQLAlchemy + Alembic.
All endpoints require auth middleware.
</reviewer_context_note>
```

Stack detection logic (priority: config files > package managers > extensions):
| Files | Stack | Default Review Agents |
|-------|-------|-----------------------|
| Gemfile + config/routes.rb | rails | kieran-rails-reviewer, dhh-rails-reviewer, code-simplicity-reviewer, security-sentinel, performance-oracle |
| Gemfile only | ruby | kieran-rails-reviewer, code-simplicity-reviewer, security-sentinel, performance-oracle |
| tsconfig.json | typescript | kieran-typescript-reviewer, code-simplicity-reviewer, security-sentinel, performance-oracle |
| package.json (no TS dep) | javascript | kieran-typescript-reviewer, code-simplicity-reviewer, security-sentinel, performance-oracle |
| pyproject.toml or requirements.txt | python | kieran-python-reviewer, code-simplicity-reviewer, security-sentinel, performance-oracle |
| None | general | code-simplicity-reviewer, security-sentinel, performance-oracle, architecture-strategist |

Monorepo detection: check for pnpm-workspace.yaml, lerna.json, go.work. If found, store stacks as array.

Interactive flow:
1. Check if project-setup.md exists -> offer reconfigure/view/cancel
2. Auto-detect stack -> let user override
3. Present 3 profiles: Strict (few agents, fast), Balanced (default), Comprehensive (all agents)
4. Show selected agents -> let user toggle on/off
5. Optional: add review context notes
6. Write config file

### Research Insights

**Best Practices (from project-setup researcher):**
- Use include strategy (whitelist) with sensible defaults, allow exclude override
- Structure Review Context with explicit sections (Project Goals, Constraints, Architecture, Code Style)
- Add version field to YAML for schema evolution
- Cache detection for session (no re-discovery between commands)

**Security (CRITICAL - from security-sentinel):**
- Review Context field is a direct prompt injection path
- MUST wrap in <reviewer_context_note> XML tag (not raw interpolation)
- Length-limit to 500 characters
- Strip prompt-structural characters (<, >, SYSTEM:, ASSISTANT:, triple backticks)
- Document commit-vs-gitignore decision explicitly for teams

**Architecture (from architecture-strategist):**
- .beads/config/ is the correct location (not .beads/memory/)
- Update provision-memory.sh to also provision .beads/config/
- Add .beads/config/ to protected artifacts in beads-review.md
- Config-missing = dispatch all agents (backward compatible, no degradation)

**Edge Cases (from project-setup researcher):**
- TypeScript in Rails project: detect both stacks, store as array
- Multiple Python packages: pyproject.toml + setup.py = single Python stack
- Monorepo with frontend/backend: per-workspace stack detection

## Testing

- [ ] SKILL.md created with proper format
- [ ] Stack detection works for each supported stack
- [ ] Monorepo detection works (pnpm-workspace.yaml, lerna.json, go.work)
- [ ] Default agent lists are correct per stack
- [ ] Config file written to .beads/config/project-setup.md (NOT .beads/memory/)
- [ ] Existing config detected and offered for reconfiguration
- [ ] All 14 review agents available for selection
- [ ] Review Context wrapped in <reviewer_context_note> XML tag
- [ ] Review Context length-limited to 500 chars
- [ ] Review Context stripped of prompt-structural characters
- [ ] .beads/config/ directory created by installer if missing

## Validation

- [ ] Skill directory created at plugins/beads-compound/skills/project-setup/
- [ ] SKILL.md follows existing skill format conventions
- [ ] Config uses YAML frontmatter + XML-wrapped Review Context body
- [ ] Config stored in .beads/config/ not .beads/memory/
- [ ] Integration point documented for beads-review consumption
- [ ] Security: no raw interpolation of Review Context into prompts

- plugins/beads-compound/skills/project-setup/SKILL.md (new)

None — independent of other beads

## What

Enhance /beads-plan with two upstream improvements:

### Sources section (mandatory)
All plans must include a Sources section in the epic bead description listing:
- Referenced files and documents (with line numbers where relevant)
- Related brainstorms (bead ID + title + locked decisions)
- Knowledge entries from knowledge.jsonl (key + type + relevance)
- External documentation URLs from research phase
- Research findings snapshot (patterns discovered, risk areas)

Add Sources to:
- Epic bead description template (Step 5)
- Child bead description template (as a References subsection)
- Pre-submission checklist (Step 6)

### Cross-check validation (non-blocking warnings)
After creating all child beads (Step 5), before the final review (Step 6), add a warning-only validation step:

Structural warnings:
- Epic has 2+ child beads (flag if too few or > 8)
- Child bead descriptions include What/Context/Testing/Validation/Files/Dependencies
- No two independent child beads modify the same files without a dependency
- Research findings captured as comments (INVESTIGATION/PATTERN/FACT)

Format:
```
Cross-Check Results for {EPIC_ID}

! WARNING: {CHILD_ID} lacks "Files" section
! WARNING: {CHILD_1} and {CHILD_2} both modify src/auth/* without dependency
v PASS: All child beads have Testing and Validation sections
v PASS: DAG validation passes (bd swarm validate)

-> Proceed to final review, or fix warnings first?
```

DO NOT add: word count checks, naming convention checks, YAGNI compliance (these are reviewer judgment, not formula).

## Context

Upstream calls this the "plan-validate-execute" pattern. Sources section ensures traceability — "where did that assumption come from?" has an answer 6 months later.

### Research Insights

**Best Practices (from plan validation researcher):**
- Sources format: use structured subsections (Brainstorm Reference, Key Files, Knowledge Entries, External Research)
- Non-blocking validation is industry standard (Linear, Notion, Jira all use warnings, not errors)
- Cross-check catches real problems (file conflicts, missing sections) without blocking the happy path
- Errors reserved for schema violations only

**Simplicity (from code-simplicity-reviewer):**
- Sources section: low-cost, high-value — keep
- Cross-check validation: the existing pre-submission checklist (Step 6) and bd swarm validate already cover most checks
- Counterpoint: the warning-based cross-check catches file overlap issues BEFORE swarm validate, which only checks DAG structure. The file overlap check is net-new value.
- Keep cross-check but scope it to high-value checks only (file overlaps, missing required sections)

**Architecture (from architecture-strategist):**
- Cross-check fits the existing SpecFlow analysis pattern (beads-plan Step 3)
- Additive change to existing command, no structural concerns

## Testing

- [ ] Sources listed in pre-submission checklist
- [ ] Cross-check warnings run after bead creation (before Step 6)

- [ ] Cross-check catches file overlap without deps (warning, not error)
- [ ] Cross-check does NOT block plan creation (warnings only)

- [ ] Cross-check validation phase added between Steps 5 and 6
- [ ] Pre-submission checklist includes Sources verification
- [ ] Backward compatible with existing plans
- [ ] No false-positive warnings for valid plans

Enhance the brainstorm detection in beads-plan Step 0 with tighter integration:

1. Check if the input bead itself has a brainstorm label (beads-brainstorm adds --labels=brainstorm)

**Simplicity (from code-simplicity-reviewer):**
- This is a small enhancement, not a standalone bead-sized task
- Two concrete changes: (a) add label check, (b) add Sources entry

## What

Enhance beads-parallel to read project-setup config (.beads/config/project-setup.md) and use it to inject relevant agent guidance into subagent prompts during execution.

IMPORTANT: This bead was RESCOPED based on review findings. The original plan for filesystem scanning of ~/.claude/agents/ was dropped due to security risks (prompt injection from arbitrary files) and complexity concerns. Instead, this bead uses the project-setup config as the source of agent relevance.

Implementation:
- In Section 8 (Recall Knowledge), also read .beads/config/project-setup.md if it exists
- Extract the review_agents list and Review Context
- For each bead in the wave, inject the Review Context into the subagent prompt under a new "## Project Context" section, wrapped in <reviewer_context_note> XML tags
- This gives subagents awareness of project conventions without runtime filesystem scanning

This is a lightweight integration: read one config file, inject its Review Context into prompts. No agent discovery, no frontmatter parsing, no matching algorithm.

## Context

### Research Insights — Why the Original Plan Was Dropped

**Security (CRITICAL - from security-sentinel):**
- ~/.claude/agents/ is outside the project and writable by ANY process (npm postinstall, etc.)
- Arbitrary .md files from disk injected into prompts = prompt injection vector
- No file size bounds in shell-based runtime (TypeScript security.ts limits don't apply)
- Recommendation: restrict to project-local .claude/agents/ only, or drop entirely

**Architecture (from architecture-strategist):**
- Runtime filesystem scanning creates tight coupling between execution engine and agent format
- Unbounded prompt growth from discovered agents
- Users who want custom agents can already put guidance in CLAUDE.md or bead descriptions

**Simplicity (from code-simplicity-reviewer):**
- Dynamic agent discovery fails YAGNI — no current user complaint about missing custom agents
- Frontmatter parsing in bash is fragile
- Match heuristic will be wrong often enough to be noise

**Rescoped approach:**
- Read project-setup config (already created by bead .1)
- Inject Review Context into subagent prompts (passive context, not instructions)
- Zero filesystem scanning, zero agent matching, zero security risk

## Testing

- [ ] With project-setup config: Review Context injected into subagent prompts
- [ ] Without config: no change to existing behavior
- [ ] Review Context wrapped in <reviewer_context_note> XML tags
- [ ] Works in default, --ralph, and --teams modes

description:
## What

Create a new review agent at plugins/beads-compound/agents/review/migration-drift-detector.md that detects unrelated schema/migration changes in PRs across multiple ORMs.

Core algorithm (ORM-agnostic):
1. Detect which ORM is in use by checking project files
2. List all migration files in the PR diff
3. List all schema artifact changes in the PR diff
4. Cross-reference: every schema change must be caused by a migration in the PR
5. Flag "drift" when schema items appear that aren't associated with PR migrations

ORM-specific adapters:

| Stack | Migration Path | Schema Artifact | Version Detection | Drift Signal |
|-------|---------------|-----------------|-------------------|-------------|
| Rails | db/migrate/*.rb | db/schema.rb | Timestamp in filename | schema.rb version > max migration version |
| Alembic | alembic/versions/*.py | SQLAlchemy model files | Revision ID (DAG, not timestamp) | Multiple heads without merge, operations not in revision chain |
| Prisma | prisma/migrations/*/migration.sql | prisma/schema.prisma | Timestamp dir name | Checksum mismatch, shadow DB divergence |
| Drizzle | drizzle/*/migration.sql | drizzle/meta/*.snapshot.json | Timestamp in filename | Snapshot diff not matching migration SQL |
| Knex | migrations/*.js or *.ts | N/A (no schema file) | Timestamp in filename | Missing files for applied versions, out-of-sequence timestamps |

Auto-detection: Agent reads project files to determine which ORM is in use, then applies the correct adapter logic.

Output: Clean report listing matched vs drifted changes, with actionable fix instructions per ORM.

## Context

Upstream has a Rails-only schema-drift-detector. We generalize it to support multiple ORMs.

### Research Insights

**Migration Drift Research (detailed per-ORM findings):**

**Rails:**
- schema.rb is auto-generated, version in header = latest migration timestamp
- If schema.rb version > max PR migration version = drift from other branches
- Check each table/column/index in schema.rb diff has corresponding migration
- Strong_migrations gem catches unsafe operations (complementary tool)

**Alembic (Python):**
- Uses DAG (directed acyclic graph), NOT simple timestamps like Rails
- down_revision links revisions together (can create merge commits)
- Multiple heads = branching (may be intentional, not always drift)
- Autogenerate has false positives: empty string vs None, VARCHAR vs TEXT synonyms
- Check DAG acyclicity, verify operations in revision chain

**Prisma:**
- SHA-256 checksums in _prisma_migrations table detect modified files
- Shadow database approach ensures reproducible drift detection
- False positives: FK constraints on existing DB, Supabase extension naming
- Implicit many-to-many tables create hidden tables (false positive risk)

**Drizzle:**
- JSON snapshots in drizzle/meta/ provide deterministic diffing
- _journal.json tracks migration order and snapshot references
- SHA256 hash in __drizzle_migrations detects modified files

**Knex:**
- NO auto-generated schema file — must validate migration files only
- Timestamp-based ordering, batch numbering for rollback
- Check: missing files for applied versions, out-of-sequence timestamps

**Security (HIGH - from security-sentinel):**
- All filenames from PR metadata must be sanitized before shell commands
- Allowlist character class: [A-Za-z0-9._/-]
- Always quote shell variables
- Follow kb_insert CSV import pattern for any SQL queries
- Never interpolate external strings into SQL literals

**Simplicity (from code-simplicity-reviewer):**
- Alternative considered: start Rails-only, extend later
- Counterpoint (from architecture-strategist): the detection pattern is the same across ORMs. Splitting creates 5 agents that are 80% identical. One agent with ORM-specific branches is cleaner.
- Decision: keep multi-ORM but use a dispatch pattern (detect ORM, run adapter)

**Common False Positive Avoidance:**
- Auto-generated schema updates with corresponding migrations = NOT drift
- Database extension changes (Prisma on Supabase) = use baseline approach
- Type synonyms (Alembic VARCHAR vs TEXT) = dialect-aware comparison
- Multiple PR migrations with dependencies = verify DAG before flagging

## Testing

- [ ] Agent created with proper frontmatter
- [ ] Rails detection: db/migrate/ + db/schema.rb
- [ ] Alembic detection: alembic/versions/ (DAG validation, not just timestamps)
- [ ] Prisma detection: prisma/migrations/ + checksum validation
- [ ] Drizzle detection: drizzle/ + snapshot comparison
- [ ] Knex detection: migrations/ (file presence + ordering only)
- [ ] Cross-reference logic flags drift correctly per ORM
- [ ] Clean PRs reported as clean (no false positives)
- [ ] Multiple ORMs in same project: detect primary ORM from PR files
- [ ] Filenames sanitized before shell commands (allowlist: [A-Za-z0-9._/-])

## Validation

- [ ] Agent file at plugins/beads-compound/agents/review/migration-drift-detector.md
- [ ] Follows existing agent format conventions (see other review agents)
- [ ] ORM auto-detection works without configuration
- [ ] Output format is actionable with fix instructions
- [ ] No shell injection from PR-sourced filenames

## Files

- plugins/beads-compound/agents/review/migration-drift-detector.md (new)

## Dependencies

None — independent of other beads

description:
Create plugins/beads-compound/opencode/plugin.ts — a TypeScript plugin for OpenCode. Bun executes TypeScript natively, so there is no build step.

## Scope

Port auto-recall.sh and memory-capture.sh only. Subagent wrapup handled in bdcompound-0k4.

## Requirements

### Plugin Structure
- Import Plugin type from @opencode-ai/plugin (pin exact version in package.json)
- Destructure { $, directory, worktree } from plugin context
- Create package.json with pinned @opencode-ai/plugin dependency

### Event Mapping
1. **session.created** -> auto-recall.sh
   - Export CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR=directory before calling
   - Parse stdout hookSpecificOutput JSON from auto-recall.sh

2. **tool.execute.after** (filter: tool === "bash") -> memory-capture.sh
   - CRITICAL PERFORMANCE: Filter bd comments? add pattern in TypeScript BEFORE spawning subprocess (95% of calls are no-ops, avoid subprocess overhead)
   - Use Bun.spawn() with stdin piping, NOT $ shell template (security: prevents command injection)
   - Translate OpenCode payload to Claude Code stdin format ({tool_name: "Bash", tool_input: {command: "..."}, cwd: "..."})

3. **shell.env** hook for environment bridging

description:
Create conversion scripts that installers invoke to transform .claude/ files into platform-specific formats.

## Architecture

**Source of truth:** .claude/commands/, .claude/skills/, .claude/agents/

**Conversion scripts (TypeScript + Bun):**
- scripts/convert-opencode.ts
- scripts/convert-gemini.ts

**Installers invoke conversions:**
```bash
./install.sh -opencode /path/to/project
  → bun run scripts/convert-opencode.ts
  → Copy converted files to /path/to/project/.opencode/

./install.sh -gemini /path/to/project
  → bun run scripts/convert-gemini.ts
  → Copy converted files to /path/to/project/.gemini/
```

## Conversion Requirements

### convert-opencode.ts

**Commands:**
- Input: .claude/commands/*.md
- Output: .opencode/commands/*.md
- Changes: None needed (same format)
- Action: Direct copy

**Skills:**
- Input: .claude/skills/*/SKILL.md
- Output: .opencode/skills/*/SKILL.md
- Changes: None needed (OpenCode searches .claude/skills/ automatically)
- Action: Optional copy or skip (document that OpenCode finds them)

**Agents:**
- Input: .claude/agents/*.md with frontmatter (name, description, model)
- Output: .opencode/agents/*.md with frontmatter (description, mode, model, tools, permission)
- Changes:
  - Remove 'name' field (use filename)
  - Add 'mode: subagent' (default)
  - Map model: 'sonnet' → 'anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-20250514'
  - Add default tools/permission fields
- Logic: Parse YAML, transform fields, write new file

**MCP Servers:**
- Input: .mcp.json
- Output: Add to opencode.json mcpServers section
- Action: Document for user (can't auto-merge into their config)

### convert-gemini.ts

**Commands:**
- Input: .claude/commands/*.md with YAML frontmatter
- Output: .gemini/commands/*.toml
- Changes:
  - Extract description from YAML frontmatter
  - Convert body: $ARGUMENTS → {{args}}
  - Output TOML format:
    ```toml
    prompt = """
    <body with {{args}}>
    """
    description = "<description>"
    ```

**Skills:**
- Input: .claude/skills/*/SKILL.md
- Output: .gemini/skills/*/SKILL.md
- Changes: None needed (same format)
- Action: Direct copy

**Agents:**
- Input: .claude/agents/*.md with frontmatter (name, description, model)
- Output: .gemini/agents/*.md with frontmatter (name, description, kind, tools, model, temperature, max_turns)
- Changes:
  - Keep 'name' field
  - Keep 'description' field
  - Add 'kind: local' (default)
  - Map model: 'sonnet' → 'gemini-2.5-pro' (or keep Claude model?)
  - Add default max_turns, timeout_mins
- Logic: Parse YAML, transform fields, write new file

**MCP Servers:**
- Input: .mcp.json
- Output: settings.json mcpServers section
- Action: Document for user

## Implementation

### scripts/convert-opencode.ts
```typescript
import { parse, stringify } from 'yaml';
import { readdir, readFile, writeFile, mkdir } from 'fs/promises';
import { join } from 'path';

// Convert commands (direct copy)
async function convertCommands() {
  const commands = await readdir('plugins/beads-compound/commands');
  await mkdir('plugins/beads-compound/opencode/commands', { recursive: true });
  for (const cmd of commands) {
    const content = await readFile(`plugins/beads-compound/commands/${cmd}`, 'utf-8');
    await writeFile(`plugins/beads-compound/opencode/commands/${cmd}`, content);
  }
}

// Convert agents (field mapping)
async function convertAgents() {
  const agents = await readdir('plugins/beads-compound/agents', { recursive: true });
  for (const agentPath of agents.filter(f => f.endsWith('.md'))) {
    const content = await readFile(agentPath, 'utf-8');
    const [, frontmatter, body] = content.match(/^---\n(.*?)\n---\n(.*)$/s);
    const yaml = parse(frontmatter);
    
    const openCodeYaml = {
      description: yaml.description,
      mode: 'subagent',
      model: mapModel(yaml.model),
      // Add default tools/permissions
    };
    
    const output = `---\n${stringify(openCodeYaml)}---\n${body}`;
    await writeFile(`plugins/beads-compound/opencode/agents/${basename(agentPath)}`, output);
  }
}

function mapModel(claudeModel: string): string {
  const mapping = {
    'sonnet': 'anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-20250514',
    'opus': 'anthropic/claude-opus-4-6',
    'haiku': 'anthropic/claude-haiku-4-5-20251001'
  };
  return mapping[claudeModel] || claudeModel;
}
```

### scripts/convert-gemini.ts
Similar structure but outputs .toml for commands and maps to Gemini agent fields.

## Testing

Create test fixtures and verify:
1. Commands convert correctly (.md → .md for OpenCode, .md → .toml for Gemini)
2. Agents map fields correctly
3. Skills copy correctly
4. Output files are valid YAML/TOML

## Documentation

Update README.md:
- Note Bun dependency for OpenCode/Gemini installation
- Show conversion commands
- Explain what gets converted

## GitHub Action (Optional - Future Enhancement)

Create .github/workflows/release.yml to:
1. Run conversion scripts
2. Create platform-specific bundles
3. Attach to GitHub release

This allows users to download pre-converted bundles without cloning repo.
notes:
## SECURITY REQUIREMENTS (from plan review)

### Critical Security Controls

1. **Path Traversal Protection**
   - Validate all file paths before read/write operations
   - Ensure resolved paths stay within expected directories
   - Sanitize filenames: `filename.replace(/[^a-zA-Z0-9._-]/g, '_')`
   - Use path validation:
     ```typescript
     function validatePath(basePath: string, targetPath: string): string {
       const resolved = resolve(basePath, targetPath);
       const rel = relative(basePath, resolved);
       if (rel.startsWith('..') || resolve(basePath, rel) \!== resolved) {
         throw new Error(`Path traversal detected: ${targetPath}`);
       }
       return resolved;
     }
     ```

2. **File Size Limits**
   - Enforce 10MB maximum file size to prevent DoS
   - Check before reading files:
     ```typescript
     const MAX_FILE_SIZE = 10 * 1024 * 1024;
     const stats = await stat(path);
     if (stats.size > MAX_FILE_SIZE) {
       throw new Error(`File too large: ${path}`);
     }
     ```

3. **YAML Parsing Security**
   - Use SAFE_SCHEMA to prevent code execution
   - Pin js-yaml to exact version 4.1.0 (no ^ or ~)
   - Validate frontmatter structure:
     ```typescript
     import { load as yamlLoad } from 'js-yaml';
     const parsed = yamlLoad(frontmatter, {
       schema: 'SAFE_SCHEMA',
       json: true
     });
     ```

4. **Template Injection Prevention (Gemini)**
   - Escape existing {{ }} syntax before conversion
   - Validate context (not inside code blocks)
   - Safe substitution:
     ```typescript
     let safe = content
       .replace(/\{\{/g, '\\{\\{')
       .replace(/\}\}/g, '\\}\\}');
     return safe.replace(/\$ARGUMENTS(?\!\w)/g, '{{args}}');
     ```

5. **File Permissions Hardening**
   - Set restrictive umask before operations: `umask 077`
   - Explicit permissions on created files:
     ```typescript
     await writeFile(path, content, { mode: 0o644 });
     await chmod(path, 0o644); // Ensure permissions set
     ```

6. **Model Name Validation**
   - Strict alphanumeric + hyphen validation
   - Allowlist of supported models
   - Reject invalid formats:
     ```typescript
     if (\!/^[a-z0-9-]+$/i.test(input)) {
       throw new Error(`Invalid model name: ${input}`);
     }
     ```

7. **Bun Runtime Verification**
   - Pin Bun to exact version in package.json
   - Use frozen lockfile: `bun install --frozen-lockfile`
   - Pin dependencies exactly (no ^ or ~):
     ```json
     {
       "devDependencies": {
         "@opencode-ai/plugin": "1.0.0",
         "js-yaml": "4.1.0"
       }
     }
     ```

### Implementation Checklist

- [ ] Path validation function implemented
- [ ] File size checks on all reads
- [ ] YAML SAFE_SCHEMA usage
- [ ] Template escaping for Gemini conversion
- [ ] Explicit file permissions (644/755)
- [ ] Model name allowlist validation
- [ ] Dependencies pinned to exact versions
- [ ] Filename sanitization

### Testing Requirements

- [ ] Test path traversal attempts (../../../etc/passwd)
- [ ] Test oversized files (>10MB)
- [ ] Test malicious YAML payloads
- [ ] Test template injection in Gemini conversion
- [ ] Verify file permissions after creation

When user selects 'Apply feedback' after a plan review, the agent has no instructions for how to enumerate, apply, and verify all recommendations. The result is that some recommendations get silently skipped, and the user believes everything was applied when it wasn't.

Fix: Replace the vague 'Apply feedback' option with a rigorous application protocol that:

3. Does a completeness check before reporting done - comparing applied changes against the original list

Also needs to cover beads-deepen which has a similar gap: updates child beads but no verification that all agent recommendations were actually incorporated.

description:
Add Gemini CLI support via settings.json hook mapping and gemini-extension.json manifest. Gemini CLI uses the same stdin/stdout JSON protocol as Claude Code, so our existing shell scripts should work with minimal changes.

## Research Findings

Gemini CLI has a mature hook system nearly identical to Claude Code:
- JSON on stdin, JSON on stdout (same wire protocol)
- Built-in Claude Code migration path in packages/cli/src/commands/hooks/migrate.ts
- Configured in settings.json at project/user/system level
- Extensions install from GitHub: `gemini extensions install <github-url>`

### Event Mapping
| Feature | Claude Code | Gemini CLI |
|---------|------------|------------|
| Session start | SessionStart | SessionStart |
| After tool use | PostToolUse | AfterTool |
| Before tool use | PreToolUse | BeforeTool |
| Agent completion | SubagentStop | AfterAgent |
| Session end | - | SessionEnd |

### Reads GEMINI.md
Gemini CLI reads GEMINI.md (not AGENTS.md), but the filename is configurable via context.fileName setting.

## Implementation

### Step 1: Create gemini-extension.json manifest
Create a gemini-extension.json at the plugin root so users can install directly from GitHub:
`gemini extensions install https://github.com/roberto-mello/beads-compound-plugin`

The manifest should declare:
- Extension name, version, description
- Hook registrations (SessionStart, AfterTool, AfterAgent)
- Required files (shell scripts from hooks/)

### Step 2: Create Gemini CLI settings.json snippet
Create a settings.json hook configuration that maps Gemini events to our shell scripts:
- SessionStart -> auto-recall.sh
- AfterTool (matcher: bash/shell) -> memory-capture.sh
- AfterAgent -> subagent-wrapup.sh (if applicable)

### Step 3: Verify shell script compatibility
Test that auto-recall.sh, memory-capture.sh, and subagent-wrapup.sh work when called by Gemini CLI. The stdin JSON format may differ slightly in field names (e.g. tool_name vs tool field).

### Step 4: Handle any stdin JSON differences
If Gemini CLI sends different field names than Claude Code, add a thin translation layer in the shell scripts or create a wrapper script.

### Step 5: Document setup
Add Gemini CLI section to README with setup instructions. Users install via:
`gemini extensions install https://github.com/roberto-mello/beads-compound-plugin`

## Files to create/modify
- plugins/beads-compound/gemini-extension.json (extension manifest for GitHub install)
- plugins/beads-compound/gemini/settings.json (hook configuration snippet)
- Section in main README for Gemini CLI setup
- Possibly a thin wrapper script if stdin JSON translation is needed

## Estimated effort
~30-40 lines of configuration + manifest + testing.

description:
## Summary

Replace grep-based knowledge recall with SQLite FTS5 full-text search while keeping JSONL as a portable export format. The recall benchmark (plugins/beads-compound/tests/recall-bench.sh) proved FTS5 outperforms grep across all metrics on 25 curated queries against 167 real knowledge entries from medsimples:

- Precision@5: 0.525 vs 0.445 (+18%)
- Recall@5: 0.653 vs 0.559 (+17%)
- MRR: 0.760 vs 0.613 (+24%)

Biggest wins: conceptual/multi-word queries where grep returns zero results (webhook auth, credits, env vars). Grep still wins on short abbreviations (RLS) where substring matching helps.

## Architecture

SQLite is authoritative, JSONL is the export (mirrors beads' own sync pattern):

- Capture: memory-capture.sh INSERT into knowledge.db, APPEND to knowledge.jsonl
- Recall: auto-recall.sh FTS5 MATCH query with BM25 ranking, grep fallback if db missing
- Manual search: recall.sh uses FTS5 when available, grep otherwise
- Backfill: first session imports 276 knowledge-prefixed comments from beads.db + existing JSONL

## Key Design Decisions

- Separate knowledge.db in .beads/memory/ (not inside beads.db) to avoid coupling to beads internals
- sqlite3 CLI for hook operations (no Python dependency in hot path, sqlite3 always available)
- Porter stemmer + unicode61 tokenizer for Portuguese content and stemming
- BM25 weights: content=10, tags=5, type=2, key=1 (validated by benchmark)
- One-time idempotent backfill via .backfill_done marker file

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] knowledge-db.sh library with kb_ensure_db, kb_insert, kb_search, kb_backfill functions
- [ ] memory-capture.sh dual-writes to SQLite + JSONL
- [ ] auto-recall.sh uses FTS5 search with grep fallback
- [ ] recall.sh uses FTS5 for keyword search with grep fallback  
- [ ] install.sh copies knowledge-db.sh to both .claude/hooks/ and .beads/memory/
- [ ] uninstall.sh removes knowledge-db.sh (preserves knowledge.db data)
- [ ] Backfill imports from beads.db comments + existing JSONL on first run
- [ ] Benchmark scores match or exceed: P@5=0.525, R@5=0.653, MRR=0.760
- [ ] Graceful fallback to grep when sqlite3 unavailable or knowledge.db missing

## Validation

1. Fresh install test: install into /tmp/test-project, verify knowledge-db.sh deployed
2. Capture test: bd comments add triggers dual-write to .db and .jsonl
3. Backfill test: first session on medsimples creates knowledge.db with >= 167 entries
4. Search quality: "webhook authentication" and "environment variables" return relevant results (both were grep=0)
5. Benchmark regression: recall-bench.sh FTS5 scores stable
6. Fallback test: rm knowledge.db, recall.sh still works via grep

## Plan File

Full implementation plan at: .claude/plans/zany-riding-galaxy.md
title: Hybrid JSONL + SQLite FTS5 knowledge recall system

## Functions

- kb_ensure_db DB_PATH — create knowledge.db with FTS5 schema if missing
- kb_insert DB_PATH JSON_ENTRY — insert a knowledge entry (INSERT OR IGNORE by key)
- kb_search DB_PATH QUERY TOP_N — FTS5 MATCH with BM25 ranking, return formatted results
- kb_backfill DB_PATH KNOWLEDGE_JSONL — one-time import from beads.db comments + existing JSONL

## Schema

Same as proven in benchmark (tests/build-index.sh):
- knowledge table: key, type, content, source, tags_text, ts, bead
- knowledge_fts FTS5: content, tags_text, type, key with porter unicode61 tokenizer
- INSERT/DELETE/UPDATE triggers to keep FTS in sync

## Implementation Notes

- Use sqlite3 CLI (not Python) — hooks must be fast, sqlite3 always available
- SQL escaping: pipe JSON fields through jq, use sqlite3 .parameter or heredoc approach
- kb_backfill: check .backfill_done marker, import from beads.db comments WHERE text LIKE 'LEARNED:%' OR ..., then import existing JSONL entries, touch marker
- kb_search: extract terms, quote each, join with OR, execute FTS5 MATCH with bm25() ordering
- BM25 weights: content=-10, tags=-5, type=-2, key=-1

This is the foundation — all other child beads depend on this library existing.

- [ ] kb_search returns results ranked by BM25 relevance

description:
Create a command that allows users to manually trigger knowledge recall mid-session without restarting Claude Code.

## Problem
Currently, knowledge recall only happens automatically at SessionStart via the auto-recall.sh hook. If users are in an ad-hoc session (not using /beads-plan workflow), they have no easy way to search and inject relevant knowledge mid-session. The only option is to restart the session or run the awkward bash command: `bash .beads/memory/recall.sh "keyword"`

## Solution
Add /beads-recall command that:
- Searches knowledge.jsonl and injects relevant context as markdown
- Supports multiple input modes (keywords, bead IDs, topics, recent entries)
- Follows existing beads command patterns (lightweight utility, no multi-agent orchestration)
- Uses recall.sh script directly

## Research Completed
Task agent analyzed existing commands (beads-checkpoint, beads-compound, beads-work) and identified patterns:
- Frontmatter: use disable-model-invocation: true (lightweight utility)
- Output: markdown format, not JSON
- Integration: direct bash execution of recall.sh
- Input handling: detect bead ID pattern vs keywords
- Error handling: graceful fallbacks for missing files/beads

## Implementation Approach
1. Create plugins/beads-compound/commands/beads-recall.md
2. Follow patterns from beads-checkpoint (lightweight, direct bash)
3. Support recall.sh modes: keywords, --recent N, --stats, --topic BD-XXX, --type TYPE
4. Parse arguments to detect bead IDs and extract context
5. Format output consistently with other commands
6. Add to README command table

## Success Criteria
- Users can run /beads-recall "oauth" to get relevant knowledge mid-session
- Supports /beads-recall BD-001 to recall knowledge about specific bead
- Supports /beads-recall --recent 10 for latest entries
- Supports /beads-recall --stats for knowledge base statistics
- Output format matches other beads commands
- Command listed in README and plugin.json
title: Add /beads-recall command for mid-session knowledge lookup

description:
## Issue
Memory directory setup logic is duplicated across 3 files:
1. auto-recall.sh (lines 34-57) - native plugin bootstrap
2. check-memory.sh (lines 45-58) - global install auto-detect
3. install.sh (lines 134-151) - manual per-project install

Each has slightly different behavior (e.g., .gitattributes location differs).

## Why This Matters
Future changes to provisioning must be applied in 3 places. The .gitattributes inconsistency is already a concrete divergence.

## Suggested Fix
Create hooks/provision-memory.sh with a setup_memory_directory() function that all 3 scripts source.

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] Single source of truth for memory directory creation
- [ ] All 3 callers produce identical .beads/memory/ structure
- [ ] .gitattributes placement consistent across all paths
title: Extract shared memory provisioning into provision-memory.sh

description:
## Issue
auto-recall.sh writes .gitattributes inside .beads/memory/ (line 52), while install.sh and check-memory.sh write to the project root .gitattributes. Both work (git respects per-directory .gitattributes), but the inconsistency can confuse users and maintainers.

## Location
plugins/beads-compound/hooks/auto-recall.sh:52-54

## Why This Matters
A user who installed via native plugin gets .beads/memory/.gitattributes, while manual install gets project root .gitattributes. Different file locations for the same merge strategy.

## Suggested Fix
Either standardize on per-directory .gitattributes (simpler, more scoped) or project root (conventional). If extracting to provision-memory.sh, make this decision once.

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] All installation paths produce .gitattributes in same location
- [ ] Union merge strategy works correctly for knowledge files
title: Fix .gitattributes placement in auto-recall.sh bootstrap

description:
## Issue
Line 57 of auto-recall.sh stages the entire .beads/memory/ directory:
```bash
(cd "$PROJECT_DIR" && git add .beads/memory/ 2>/dev/null)
```

This could inadvertently stage unexpected files if something else created files in that directory before bootstrap completed.

## Location
plugins/beads-compound/hooks/auto-recall.sh:57

## Suggested Fix
Stage only the known files explicitly:
```bash
(cd "$PROJECT_DIR" && git add .beads/memory/knowledge.jsonl .beads/memory/.gitattributes .beads/memory/recall.sh .beads/memory/knowledge-db.sh 2>/dev/null) || true
```

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] Only known bootstrap files are staged
- [ ] Error suppression does not hide real failures
title: Scope git add to specific files in auto-recall.sh bootstrap

description:
## Issue
recall.sh passes TOP_N to SQLite FTS5 LIMIT clause without validating it's numeric. A crafted argument could inject SQL:
```bash
.beads/memory/recall.sh "test" --recent "10; DELETE FROM knowledge; --"
```

## Location
plugins/beads-compound/hooks/recall.sh and knowledge-db.sh kb_search function

## Suggested Fix
Add numeric validation before use:
```bash
if ! [[ "$TOP_N" =~ ^[0-9]+$ ]]; then
  TOP_N=10
fi
```

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] TOP_N rejects non-numeric input
- [ ] Default fallback to 10 on invalid input
- [ ] No SQL injection possible via recall.sh arguments
title: Validate TOP_N as numeric in recall.sh
Fix OpenCode troubleshooting paths for plugin.ts location

description:
## What

The /beads-plan command currently only accepts feature descriptions as arguments. When a user passes a bead ID (like 'bikiniup-xhr'), the command treats it as a literal feature description and researches irrelevant topics (e.g., 'XHR security' instead of loading the bead's actual content).

## Expected Behavior

The command should:
1. Detect if the argument is a bead ID (matches pattern like 'beads-*' or other valid bead ID formats)
2. If bead ID detected, load bead details with `bd show {ID}` and extract the description
3. Use the bead's description as the feature_description for planning
4. If not a bead ID, proceed with current behavior (treat as feature description)

## Context

- User ran `/beads-plan bikiniup-xhr` expecting to plan out an existing epic
- Agent researched 'XHR security' instead of loading the bead
- This creates confusion and wastes research cycles

## Testing

- [ ] Test with epic bead ID: `/beads-plan bikiniup-xhr`
- [ ] Test with feature description: `/beads-plan "Add OAuth2 authentication"`
- [ ] Verify bead content is loaded when ID is passed
- [ ] Verify feature description is used when non-ID text is passed
- [ ] Handle invalid bead IDs gracefully

## Validation

- [ ] Command correctly identifies bead IDs vs feature descriptions
- [ ] Bead content is extracted and used for planning when ID is passed
- [ ] Original feature description behavior still works
- [ ] Error handling for invalid/non-existent bead IDs
title: Support bead ID argument in /beads-plan command
